OverTheWire BanditA few months ago, I decided to go back to the fundamentals.
Instead of jumping straight into exploit development, malware analysis, or Active Directory, I wanted to build a stronger foundation first. That foundation was Linux.
After asking around and reading recommendations from the cybersecurity community, I started OverTheWire Bandit. At first, I thought it was just another Linux challenge where I’d practice commands like ls, cd, and cat.
I couldn’t have been more wrong.
As I progressed through the levels, I realized Bandit wasn’t teaching me how to find passwords. It was teaching me how to think.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
Learning to Ask Better Questions
Looking back, the biggest change wasn’t the number of Linux commands I learned.
It was the way I approached problems.
Before Bandit, whenever I got stuck, my first instinct was to search Google for the answer.
Now, I find myself slowing down and asking questions instead.

• What files are available?

• Who owns this file?

• Why does it have these permissions?

• What script is actually running?

• Is there an automated process behind this?
Those questions often lead me much further than searching for a solution immediately.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
Linux Started Making Sense
I’ve been using Linux for quite a while, but if I’m being honest, I didn’t really understand what was happening behind the scenes.
I knew directories like /etc, /tmp, and /usr/bin, but they were just paths I memorized.
Bandit changed that.
Little by little, everything started to connect.
I learned why configuration files live inside /etc, why temporary files are written to /tmp, how cron jobs automate tasks, and why file ownership and permissions are so important.
Instead of memorizing Linux, I finally started understanding it.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
Bash Wasn’t as Scary as I Expected
Coming from Python and JavaScript, Bash looked… strange.
The syntax felt unfamiliar.
do
done
then
fi
Variables prefixed with $
It all looked confusing at first.
But after writing a few small scripts, I realized Bash wasn’t trying to be another programming language.
Its job is different.
Bash exists to control the operating system.
Once I understood that, the syntax became much easier to follow.
I started using loops, pipes, redirections, command substitution, and simple automation. These may seem like small things, but they completely changed how I interact with Linux.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
The Levels That Changed My Perspective
A few challenges really stood out to me.
One of them introduced Netcat.
Before that, client-server communication was mostly theoretical in my mind. I understood the concept, but I had never actually seen it happen.
Bandit made me interact with services over TCP, send data through sockets, and understand what was happening instead of simply reading about it.
Another memorable moment was working with cron jobs.
Instead of searching directly for a password, I had to read a Bash script, understand its logic, and follow the execution flow.
That challenge completely changed the way I looked at Linux automation.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
Cybersecurity Isn’t Just About Exploits
Before starting Bandit, I had the same misconception many beginners have.
I thought cybersecurity was mostly about running tools or finding exploits.
Bandit showed me something different.
Many challenges were solved simply by understanding how Linux worked.
Reading Bash scripts.
Analyzing permissions.
Understanding scheduled tasks.
Following program logic.
Those are skills that don’t look exciting from the outside, but they’re incredibly valuable.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
The Most Valuable Skill I Gained
If someone asked me what Linux commands I learned, I could list quite a few.
But honestly, I don’t think that’s the most important takeaway.
The real lesson was learning how to investigate.
Reading documentation.
Understanding source code.
Following execution flow.
Asking “why” instead of only asking “how.”
I believe that mindset will be much more valuable as I continue learning more advanced topics.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
Final Thoughts
Completing Bandit Level 25 doesn’t make me a cybersecurity professional.
There’s still a long road ahead.
But it gave me something I didn’t have before: a much stronger understanding of Linux and, more importantly, a better way of approaching technical problems.
Now, when I see a Bash script, a cron job, or a Linux directory structure, I don’t just see commands anymore.
I try to understand why they’re designed that way.
If you’re just getting started with Linux or cybersecurity, I highly recommend giving OverTheWire Bandit a try.
Take your time.
Read the documentation.
Experiment.
And whenever possible, understand the concept before looking at a walkthrough.
The learning process is worth it.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
I’ve also documented my notes, scripts, and learning progress while completing Bandit in a GitHub repository.
GitHub Repository: https://github.com/Nyanns/OverTheWire-Bandit/tree/main
If you have feedback, suggestions, or simply want to connect with someone who’s also learning cybersecurity, I’d be happy to hear from you.
